content: Let me read the required files to understand my role and then perform the blueprint review.
I'll start by reading the required instruction files and the directive.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Under Claude Code, skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Under Claude Code, rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — under Claude Code this is installed as the `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source), providing slash commands `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc. Under other harnesses the slash-command surface is absent; the underlying skill scripts are still on disk under `.claude/skills/` and can be run directly from the shell.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts — that path is the same whatever harness you run under. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) surface as `mcp__*` tools: under Claude Code they are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json`; under other harnesses the loop wires the same servers in at launch.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned agent reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**A dispatch is a BLOCKING Bash call — wait by staying inside it.** `archon-subagent.py` is synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and returns only once the child finishes and its report is on disk). A long dispatch may be auto-backgrounded with a task ID — that's fine; wait for that task's result before acting. **Do NOT use the `Monitor` tool to wait** — it is non-blocking (returns immediately, "keep working"), so your turn ends and the subagents are abandoned mid-run. **Do NOT use foreground `sleep` / `until … ; do sleep … ; done`** — the harness blocks `sleep`. To run independent subagents **in parallel**, put all their dispatches in **one Bash call**, each backgrounded with `&`, ending with `wait` — e.g. `python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name A … & python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name B … & wait` — which blocks until all finish (the whole wave as one blocking dispatch). Serialize only subagents that write the **same file** (separate sequential calls). The rule never changes: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read (edits via `archon discuss`) |
57	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
58	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
60	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
61	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
62	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
65	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
66	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
67	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
68	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
70	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
71	
72	### Subagent permissions
73	
74	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
75	
76	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
77	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
78	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
79	
80	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
81	
82	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper.
83	
84	## Protected Declarations
85	
86	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
87	
88	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
89	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
90	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
91	
92	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
93	
94	Two semantic markers:
95	
96	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
97	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
98	
99	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
100	
101	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake build <module>` (it uses `lake build`, not `lake env lean <file>`, so dependencies are built and missing-import failures aren't spurious), and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents should NOT add or remove `\leanok` as routine bookkeeping — leave it to the sync. The one exception is the review agent: if it is **certain** the sync got a marker wrong (a clearly-finished proof left unmarked, or a marker present on something incomplete), it may manually correct it, but must explicitly surface and justify that override in its summary (see `.archon/prompts/review.md`).
102	
103	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
104	
105	## User Interaction
106	
107	The user provides hints in two places:
108	
109	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
110	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
111	
112	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
113	
114	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
115	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true or important at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
116	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
117	
118	## Agent Roles
119	
120	### Plan Agent
121	
122	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
123	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
124	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
125	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
126	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
127	- Curate `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` — the condensed, cross-iteration knowledge file injected (read-only) into every agent's prompt. Keep only durable facts that would surprise an agent reading the code fresh (dead-end tactics, files not to touch, Mathlib gap coordinates, protected invariants). Hard limits: ~10 bullets / ~600 chars; prune before adding. Do NOT duplicate what's obvious from the code or PROGRESS.md.
128	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
129	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
130	
131	### Prover Agent
132	
133	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
134	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
135	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
136	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
137	- A prover lane ends by writing its task result and exiting normally. **Do not kill Lean, Lake, shell, or sibling agent processes from inside the prover lane.** Do not run `pkill`, `killall`, regex-based process cleanup, or background-verification cleanup. If a verification command is still running, wait for its result or report that it is still running; process lifetime is owned by the Archon runner/orchestrator.
138	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
139	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
140	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
141	
142	### Subagents
143	
144	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
145	
146	### Review Agent
147	
148	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
149	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
150	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
151	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
152	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Normally leave `\leanok` to the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase; only override a marker the sync got demonstrably wrong, and justify it in your summary.
153	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
154	
155	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
156	
157	These run automatically between phases:
158	
159	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
160	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
161	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
162

1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan, dag]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
115	---
116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Dependency-graph integrity (use `leandag`)** — the `\uses{}` edges must reflect the real mathematical dependencies. `leandag` computes the actual DAG, so use it instead of eyeballing:
130	  - **Broken edges** — `leandag build --json` reports `unknown_uses` (a `\uses{}` pointing at a label no declaration defines). Every one is a must-fix.
131	  - **Missing edges** — read each proof: if it relies on a lemma/definition that its `\uses{}` does not list, the edge is missing. These don't show up as "broken"; you find them by reading the math against the declared `\uses{}`. Missing edges make the loop dispatch provers out of order, so flag them as correctness findings.
132	  - **Isolated declarations** — `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated --type theorem`, and the `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint` counts in `leandag stats`) lists nodes with **no `\uses{}` out and nothing using them**. An isolated *blueprint* node is almost always a *symptom of a missing edge*, not dead weight — so for each one decide and record a disposition (see below). Isolated `lean_aux` nodes are uncovered Lean helpers, a separate "needs a blueprint entry" signal, not removal candidates.
133	- **Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)** — it is good practice for a chapter that relies on a Mathlib-provided result to state it as an explicit anchor block marked `\mathlibok` (with `\lean{}` naming the real Mathlib declaration), rather than a bare `\uses{}` to nothing. Treat such anchors as *valid and done* — never flag them as ∞ holes or `remove` candidates. But **audit each `\mathlibok` claim for faithfulness**, because a wrong one makes the loop skip a real gap: does Mathlib actually contain the named `\lean{}` declaration, and does the stated form match it? Verify against Lean (`archon-lean-lsp`) when in doubt. A `\mathlibok` on a statement Mathlib does not provide — or whose form is stronger/different than Mathlib's — is a hard fail (treat like a fabricated citation). Also: where a broken `\uses{}` points at what is really a Mathlib result, the right `wire-up` is "add a `\mathlibok` anchor for it," not "invent a project lemma."
134	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
135	- **Rendering integrity (run `archon blueprint-doctor`)** — the blueprint must *render*: as the compiled leanblueprint site and in the dashboard. The doctor lints this deterministically — run `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once and triage every `malformed_refs` finding into your report:
136	  - **`undefined-macro`** — a chapter uses `\foo` that no `macros/*.tex` or chapter-local `\providecommand` defines: it renders as raw TeX everywhere. Recommend the definition (usually obvious from context: `\Z` → `\mathbb{Z}`, `\fppf` → `\mathrm{fppf}`); when the intent is genuinely unclear (you cannot tell what the writer meant), flag it as a correctness finding for the chapter's writer instead of guessing.
137	  - **`math-delim`** — interleaved `$ … \( … \) … $` delimiters that shred formulas mid-sentence; per-line locations are in the finding. Must-fix, writer-directive material.
138	  - **`literal-ref` / `bare-label`** — placeholder "REF" tokens and raw label ids in prose; each needs a `\cref{}` or the human-readable number.
139	  Group these per chapter in your findings so the dispatcher can scope one writer per damaged chapter.
140	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
141	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
142	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
143	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
144	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
145	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
146	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
147	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
148	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
149	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
150	
151	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
152	
153	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
154	
155	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
156	
157	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
158	
159	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
160	
161	## Always read everything
162	
163	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
164	
165	## Unstarted-phase proposals
166	
167	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
168	
169	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. (That table lists only *remaining* phases — finished ones live in `## Completed` and need no proposal.) For each remaining phase row whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
170	
171	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
172	
173	The proposal answers:
174	
175	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
176	
177	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
178	
179	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
180	
181	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
182	
183	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
184	
185	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
186	
187	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
188	
189	```
190	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
191	
192	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
193	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
194	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
195	
196	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
197	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
198	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
199	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
200	...
201	
202	**`\uses` skeleton**:
203	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
204	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
205	
206	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
207	
208	**References for writer**:
209	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
210	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
211	
212	**Subphase choices exposed**:
213	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
214	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
215	```
216	
217	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
218	
219	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
220	
221	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
222	
223	## Directive Format
224	
225	```markdown
226	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
227

229	<slug>
230	
231	## Strategy snapshot
232	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
233

235	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
236

238	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
239	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
240	
241	## Focus areas (optional)
242	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
243	
244	## Known issues
245	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
246	```
247	
248	## What you do
249	
250	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
251	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
252	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
253	   - Read the entire chapter.
254	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
255	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
256	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
257	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
258	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
259	6. **Audit the dependency graph with `leandag`.** Run `leandag build --json` (broken/unknown `\uses{}`, `unmatched_lean`, isolated count) and `leandag show isolated` once for the whole blueprint. Then triage each isolated **blueprint** node into exactly one disposition and record it:
260	   - **wire-up** — it *should* connect to the graph but an edge is missing or mislabeled. Name the `\uses{}` that should be added (and on which side). This is the common case and a correctness finding — recommend a writer fix, do NOT recommend removal.
261	   - **remove** — it is genuinely orphaned scaffolding: nothing in the goal's proof closure needs it, it isn't the goal, and it isn't a `\mathlibok`/reference anchor. Only then is removal the right call. Name the chapter + label so the plan agent can authorize a writer to delete it.
262	   - **keep** — it is intentionally standalone (the goal theorem with nothing above it, a deliberately isolated `\mathlibok` reference, etc.). Say why and move on.

267	10. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
268

270	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
271	- **`archon blueprint-doctor [--json]`** — the deterministic rendering/structure lint (orphan chapters, broken refs, literal-REF, interleaved math delimiters, bare labels, undefined macros, covers problems). Read-only; run it once per review and triage its `malformed_refs` (see "Rendering integrity" above).
272	- **`leandag`** — your read-only window into the real dependency DAG. Run it freely: it never touches project source or the blueprint. 273	  - `leandag build --json` — the structured build report: `unknown_uses` (broken `\uses{}`), `unmatched_lean` (`\lean{}` pointing nowhere), `conflicts`, and `summary.isolated`.

275	  - `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated [--type theorem] [--chapter <c>]`) — the isolated declarations, which you triage per node (below).
276	  - `leandag show gaps` — blueprint nodes missing a `\lean{}`.

278	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
279	
280	## Report format
281	
282	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md`.
283	
284	**CRITICAL COST RULE**: Your report must be extremely concise to save LLM tokens. Use dense bullet points, abbreviations, and zero conversational filler. DO NOT write paragraphs. Omit any section that has no findings. The plan agent only needs the facts.

36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}

74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}

81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}

84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}

86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,

101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)

127	
128	\begin{definition}\leanok
129	[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
130	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
131	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
132	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
133	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
134	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
135	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
136	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
137	  % L5594--5617).
138	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
139	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
140	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
141	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
142	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
143	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
145	  % between abelian categories and assume
146	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
147	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
148	  % \begin{enumerate}
149	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
150	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
151	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
152	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
153	  % \end{enumerate}"
154	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
155	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
156	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
157	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
158	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
159	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
160	  \[
161	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
162	  \]
163	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
164	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
165	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
169	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
170	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
171	
172	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
173	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
174	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
175	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
176	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
177	\end{definition}

179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}

271	  \label{lem:horseshoe_twist}

303	  \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).

312	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives

395	  Project-local supplement (the \(\tau_2\) companion of Mathlib's \(\tau_3\) transfer
396	  \texttt{quasiIso\_τ₃}). Let \(\varphi : S_1 \to S_2\) be a morphism of short exact
397	  sequences of cochain complexes, with components \(\varphi.\tau_1, \varphi.\tau_2,
398	  \varphi.\tau_3\) on the three columns. Suppose \(\varphi.\tau_1\) and \(\varphi.\tau_3\)
399	  are quasi-isomorphisms, and that the middle component \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a
400	  monomorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no incoming differential, and an
401	  epimorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no outgoing differential. Then
402	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
403	\end{lemma}
404	
405	\begin{proof}
406	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
407	  \leanok
408	  This is the homology four-lemma. Fix a degree \(i\); we show
409	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism by proving it is both epimorphic and
410	  monomorphic and concluding that a map which is both is an isomorphism. \emph{Epi:}
411	  when \(i\) has an outgoing differential to some \(j\), the two short exact sequences
412	  yield a commuting ladder whose rows are the five-term exact windows of the homology
413	  long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) about degree
414	  \(i\); the four-lemma (epi form), using that \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1)\),
415	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are epi and \(H^j(\varphi.\tau_1)\) is mono, forces
416	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) epi. When \(i\) has no outgoing differential,
417	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is epi in degree \(i\) by hypothesis, and an epimorphism of
418	  complexes that is epi in a degree with no outgoing differential is epi on homology
419	  there. \emph{Mono:} dually, when \(i\) has an incoming differential from some \(k\),
420	  the four-lemma (mono form), using that \(H^k(\varphi.\tau_3)\) is epi and
421	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1), H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are mono, forces
422	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) mono; when \(i\) has no incoming differential it is mono in
423	  that degree by hypothesis, hence mono on homology. Being epi and mono in every
424	  degree, \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), so
425	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
426	\end{proof}

515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
529	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}

531	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
532	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
533	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
534	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
535	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
536	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
537	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
538	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
539	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
540	  \[
541	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
542	  \]
543	\end{lemma}
544	
545	\begin{proof}
546	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
547	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
548	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
549	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
551	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
552	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
553	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
554	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
555	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
556	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
557	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
558	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
559	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).

618	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
619	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
620	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
621	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact

624	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
625	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
626	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
627	  complexes

630	  \]

632	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence

634	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence

636	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general

640	      \longrightarrow 0

647	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
648	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
649	  \]

651	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:

655	  sequence of right-derived functors
656	  \[

658	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,

27	\section{Setup and motivation}
28	
29	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
30	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
31	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
32	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
33	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
34	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
35	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
36	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
37	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
38	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
39	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
40	
41	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
42	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
43	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
44	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
45	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
46	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
47	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
48	\[
49	  \begin{array}{ccc}
50	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
51	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
52	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
53	  \end{array}
54	\]
55	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
56	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
57	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
58	
59	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}

177	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
178	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
179	
180	It is convenient to factor the construction of
181	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
182	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
183	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
184	
185	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
186	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
187	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
188	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
189	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
190	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
191	\[
192	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
193	    \;=\;
194	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
195	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
196	\]
197	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
198	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
199	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
200	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts

200	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
201	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
202	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
203	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
204	
205	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
206	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
207	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
208	\[
209	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
210	  \qquad
211	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
212	\]
213	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
214	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
215	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
216	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
217	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
218	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
219	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
220	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
221	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
222	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
223	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
224	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
225	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
226	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
227	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
228	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
229	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
230	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
231	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
232	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
233	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
234	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
235	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
236	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
237	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
238	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
239	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
240	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
241	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
242	
243	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
244	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
245	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
246	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
247	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
248	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
249	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
250	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
251	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
252	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
253	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
254	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
255	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
256	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
257	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
258	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
259	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
260	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
261	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
262	
263	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
264	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
265	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
266	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
267	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
